coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots-decorated N-doped Bi2MoO6 hollow nanostructures: Coupling defect engineering with interface engineering toward enhanced charge storage for supercapacitors

coredata.dc:description: The practical applications of transition-metal oxides as electrode materials for supercapacitors are still impeded by their intrinsically poor electrical conductivity and limited number of electroactive sites. Herein, a defect engineering strategy combined with interface engineering is adopted to create nitrogen-doped bismuth molybdate (N-BMO) hollow nanostructures decorated with graphene quantum dots (GQDs). Theoretical calculation and experimental results indicate that the N doping in BMO can enhance electrical conductivity of N-BMO by reducing its electronic band gap. Moreover, the surface decoration of GQDs on N-BMO enables higher electrical conductivity and more electroactive sites. More importantly, a built-in electric field is formed at the N-BMO/GQD interface due to their Fermi-level difference, which favors fast interfacial charge transfer and accelerate electrode reaction kinetics. Accordingly, the optimized N-BMO@GQD electrodes yield higher specific capacities (572 and 435 C g−1 at 1 and 10 A g−1, respectively) and better cycling stability (91 % capacity retention after 10000 cycles at 5 A g−1) compared to pristine BMO electrode. Moreover, an assembled asymmetric supercapacitor device with N-BMO@GQD as positive electrode can deliver an energy density of 45.2 Wh kg−1 at 801 W kg−1. This study showcases an efficient strategy to design and develop promising BMO-based electrode materials for supercapacitors.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the synthetic process of N-BMO@GQD hollow nanostructures.

(a) XRD patterns and (b) N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms of the prepared BMO, N-BMO, and N-BMO@GQD-2; XPS survey spectra (c), high-resolution XPS spectra of Bi 4f (d), Mo 3d (e), and O 1s (f) of the BMO, N-BMO, and N-BMO@GQD-2; (g) High-resolution N 1s spectra of the N-BMO and N-BMO@GQD-2; (h) High-resolution C 1s spectrum of N-BMO@GQD-2.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots-decorated N-doped Bi2MoO6 hollow nanostructures: Coupling defect engineering with interface engineering toward enhanced charge storage for supercapacitors

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The practical applications of transition-metal oxides as electrode materials for supercapacitors are still impeded by their intrinsically poor electrical conductivity and limited number of electroactive sites. Herein, a defect engineering strategy combined with interface engineering is adopted to create nitrogen-doped bismuth molybdate (N-BMO) hollow nanostructures decorated with graphene quantum dots (GQDs). Theoretical calculation and experimental results indicate that the N doping in BMO can enhance electrical conductivity of N-BMO by reducing its electronic band gap. Moreover, the surface decoration of GQDs on N-BMO enables higher electrical conductivity and more electroactive sites. More importantly, a built-in electric field is formed at the N-BMO/GQD interface due to their Fermi-level difference, which favors fast interfacial charge transfer and accelerate electrode reaction kinetics. Accordingly, the optimized N-BMO@GQD electrodes yield higher specific capacities (572 and 435 C g−1 at 1 and 10 A g−1, respectively) and better cycling stability (91 % capacity retention after 10000 cycles at 5 A g−1) compared to pristine BMO electrode. Moreover, an assembled asymmetric supercapacitor device with N-BMO@GQD as positive electrode can deliver an energy density of 45.2 Wh kg−1 at 801 W kg−1. This study showcases an efficient strategy to design and develop promising BMO-based electrode materials for supercapacitors.

ce:section.ce:para: The ever-increasing energy demands for portable electronics and electric vehicles have stimulated extensive research on efficient energy storage devices (ESDs). Supercapacitors (SCs) with desirable performances such as high power density, rapid discharge/charge processes, long lifetime, and low cost, have been regarded as promising candidates for next-generation ESDs [1–3]. However, compared to other ESDs such as rechargeable batteries, the low energy densities of SCs restrict their further commercial applications. Therefore, considerable endeavors have been devoted to the development of desirable electrode materials (EMs) for elevating energy densities of SCs while maintaining their high power density [4,5].

Recently, transition metal molybdates, including CoMoO4 [6], NiMoO4 [7], MnMoO4 [8], etc., have been extensively explored as EMs for SCs due to their high theoretical capacities and multiple oxidation states. Among them, Bi2MoO6 (BMO) has attracted considerable attention for their promising applications in SCs owing to its high electrochemical activity of Bi and unique aurivillius-type structures [9,10]. Nevertheless, BMO-based EMs are still subject to unsatisfactory rate capability on account of their poor inherent electrical conductivity and limited availability of electroactive sites with inadequate exposure.

To solve these problems, a defect engineering strategy, such as the heteroatom doping, is an effective approach to enhance electrical conductivity of EMs by manipulating the electronic structure to boost charge storage capability [11,12]. For example, Wu et al. fabricated N-doped NiCo2O4 nanograss on carbon fibers through a synchronously plasma-activated strategy [13]. Such N-doped NiCo2O4 electrodes afforded the largest capacity value of 1194 C g−1, which was approximately three times greater than that of undoped NiCo2O4. The doped N atoms in NiCo2O4 can better interact with Ni and Co atoms, and the formed Ni(Co)–O–N bonds possess longer bond length and higher electronegativity than the pristine Ni(Co)–O bonds. As a result, the attraction of Co2+/Co3+ (Ni2+/Ni3+) to 3d electrons is weaken, leading to an increase in electrical conductivity and an enhancement in the electrochemical activity of NiCo2O4. Hui et al. reported that the P doping in CoMoO4 caused a decrease of energy band gap as well as an increase in electrical conductivity, enabling substantially enhanced redox reaction kinetics and charge storage capacity [14]. The prepared P-doped CoMoO4 electrodes delivered a two time higher capacity value of 1368 C g−1 than pure CoMoO4 electrode (651 C g−1). Therefore, the heteroatom doping will be expected to elevate charge storage capability of BMO-based EMs in SCs, which is rarely reported in previous work.

On the other hand, integrating nanostructured metal compounds with conductive carbon-based materials has been employed to construct heterostructures with improved charge storage performance [15–19]. Among available carbonaceous materials, zero-dimensional (0D) graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have gained more attention as promising electrode materials for SCs due to their unique physicochemical properties, such as high specific surface area, abundant active sites, good electrical conductivity, and high dispersibility in various solvents [20]. Shu et al. prepared the NiCo-LDH@GQD nanocomposites by decorating GQDs onto NiCo-LDH nanosheets. This modification resulted in a significantly enhanced capacity value of 814 C g−1, surpassing the capacity of pure NiCo-LDH (534 C g−1) [21]. The surface decoration of GQDs not only decreased the band gap energy of NiCo-LDH and increased electrical conductivity but also triggered a charge redistribution at the NiCo-LDH/GQD hetero-conjunction. As a result, the NiCo-LDH@GQD nanocomposites possess dynamic advantages over that of NiCo-LDH during the electrochemical reactions. Similar results were also reported in MnO2@GQD [22] and Ni(OH)2@GQD [23] nanocomposites. Thus, it is hopeful that BMO nanostructures are combined with GQDs to construct BMO@GQD nanocomposites with improved charge storage performance.

Taking into account the above considerations, herein, N-doped BMO (N-BMO) hollow nanostructures decorated with GQDs are designed and constructed through integrating heteroatom doping with interface engineering methods. To our knowledge, there have been no reports on such nanocomposites by decorating GQDs on the surface of hollow N-BMO nanostructures. In such heterostructures, the N doping can reduce the energy band gap of BMO and increase electron mobility, thereby improving electrode reaction kinetics. On the other hand, the surface decorating of GQDs on N-BMO not only further increases electric conductivity of nanocomposites and ensures the exposure of more active sites to electrolyte, but also induces a charge distribution at the N-BMO/GQD interface, accordingly accelerating interfacial charge transfer and electrochemical activities of nanocomposites. Therefore, the N-BMO@GQD nanocomposites, when utilized as electrodes, exhibit a significantly enhanced maximum specific capacity of 572 C g−1 at 1 A g−1, surpassing the specific capacity of the unmodified BMO electrode (241 C g−1), and they still retain 76 % of the initial capacity at higher 10 A g−1. Furthermore, the assembled asymmetric supercapacitor (ASC) device with N-BMO@GQD as the positive electrode delivers the maximum energy density of 45.2 Wh kg−1 at 801 W kg−1, suggesting significant potential for practical implementation in the fields of SCs.

Bismuth nitrate pentahydrate (Bi(NO3)3·5H2O), Ammonium molybdate tetrahydrate ((NH4)6Mo7O24·4H2O), Nitric acid (HNO3, 69 wt%), and Dicyandiamide (C2H4N4) were bought from Aladdin Chemical Co., LtD. GQD powders were sourced from XFNANO (China).

First, MoO3 nanostructures as a self-template were prepared. Specifically, 0.5 g of (NH4)6Mo7O24·4H2O was dissolved in 60 mL of HNO3 aqueous solution (1.8 mol L−1). The transparent solution was then transferred into a 100 mL autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 8 h. The white MoO3 powders were acquired by filtering, washing with water, and drying at 80 °C for 12 h. Second, 50 mg of the prepared MoO3 was dispersed in 30 mL of deionized water containing 0.68 g of Bi(NO3)3·5H2O. The formed milk-like solution was then sealed in a 50 mL autoclave and heated at 120 °C for 8 h. After undergoing filtration, water washing, and drying at 80 °C for 12 h, hollow BMO nanostructures were successfully acquired.

0.1 g of the prepared hollow BMO nanostructures and 1.0 g of C2H4N4 were put in two ceramic boats, respectively, and they were transferred in a tubular furnace. The former was located in the downstream of tubular furnace, and the latter was located in the upstream of tubular furnace. Under N2 atmosphere, the samples were heated to 400 °C for 1 h, with the temperature increasing at a rate of 3 °C min−1. For comparison, the undoped BMO nanostructures was prepared under the same calcination conditions in the absence of C2H4N4.

A certain amount of GQDs (5, 10, and 20 mg) and 50 mg of N-BMO were dispersed in 15 mL of deionized water and then transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and kept at 120 °C for 4 h. After cooling to room temperature, the N-BMO@GQD nanocomposites were obtained by filtering, washing with alcohol, and drying under vacuum at 60 °C for 12 h. According to the added mass of GQDs, the corresponding products were labeled as N-BMO@GQD-1, N-BMO@GQD-2, and N-BMO@GQD-3, respectively.

The crystalline structures, surface chemical information, morphology, microstructure, and surface area of samples were analyzed by a X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8-Advance), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250Xi), scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi SU8200), transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL JEM-2100), and N2 physisorption analyzer (Quantachrome NOVA3200e), respectively. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was tested on a Shimadzu DTG-60 thermal analyzer under an air gas flow.

A CHI 660E electrochemical workstation (Chenhua Instrument Co., China) was employed to measure the cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge/discharge (GCD), and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) of samples. These electrochemical measurements were implemented in a 2 M KOH aqueous solution using a three-electrode system with saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as reference electrode, Pt wire as counter electrode, and working electrode (see details in Supporting Information). Moreover, the assembly of ASC device was also depicted in Supporting Information.

Fig. 1 provides a schematic representation of the synthesis procedure for hollow N-BMO@GQD nanocomposites. First, MoO3 nanostructures were synthesized through the hydrothermal reaction between Mo7O24 6− and H+ [24]. Second, the obtained MoO3 nanostructures as a self-template were converted in situ into 1D nanosheet-assembled hollow BMO nanostructures via the ion-exchange reaction in the presence of Bi(NO3)3. Next, the N atom was incorporated in the crystalline lattice of BMO to form hollow N-BMO nanostructures by the nitridation treatment in the presence of C2H4N4. Finally, GQDs were decorated on the surface of hollow N-BMO nanostructures through a hydrothermal treatment. The possible reactions for the synthesis of hollow BMO nanostructures are described as follows [25]:
(1) Mo7O24 6− +6H+ → 7MoO3↓ + H2O
(2) MoO3 + H2O → H2MoO4
(3) H2MoO4 → 2H+ + MoO4 2−
(4) Bi(NO3)3 → Bi3+ + 3NO3 −
(5) MoO4 2− + 2Bi3+ + 2H2O → Bi2MoO6↓ + 4H+

The morphology and microstructure of the synthesized samples were observed by SEM and TEM. Fig. S1 shows the SEM images of the synthesized MoO3, which exhibit 1D strip-like nanostructures with smooth surfaces. Their sizes are 160–500 nm in diameter and one micron to tens of microns in length. After reacting with Bi3+ ions in Bi(NO3)3 aqueous solution via the ion exchange, MoO3 nanostructures are in situ converted into BMO nanostructures. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the surface of BMO nanostructures becomes rough in comparison with that of MoO3 nanostructures (Fig. 2a). Meanwhile, the diameters of BMO nanostructures are enhanced to 500–800 nm. Close observation shows that the surface of BMO nanostructures is composed of interwoven 2D nanosheets, which grow along the radial direction and result in an increase in diameter (Fig. 2b). Particularly, there are a large number of void spaces between adjacent nanosheets, which make these nanosheets easier to fully contact with the electrolyte, thereby enhancing the electroactive sites of BMO nanostructures. Fig. 2c shows the TEM image of BMO nanostructures, which reveals a hollow structure due to the obvious contrast between the center and out parts. The shell of hollow BMO nanostructures is composed of interwoven nanosheets, which is beneficial for fast penetration of electrolyte ions into the interior, thereby accommodating more electrolyte ions and increasing the utilization of active materials [26]. Moreover, the inner open spaces help to buffer volume changes of BMO nanostructures during the charging/discharging cycles, thus enhancing structural stability.

To well understand the formation mechanism of hollow BMO nanostructures, a time-dependent morphology and microstructure evolution of hollow BMO nanostructures was investigated. Fig. S2 shows the SEM images of intermediate products collected at various reaction stages, from which one can see that the surface of MoO3 nanostructures becomes rough just after the ion-exchange reaction time for 1 h (Fig. S2a). This is because H2MoO4 molecules from the hydrolysis of MoO3 react with Bi(NO3)3 to form BMO, resulting in the appearance of interwoven BMO nanosheets on the surface of MoO3 nanostructures (Fig. S2b). The TEM image in Fig. S2c confirms the solid core of MoO3 nanostructures. With prolonging ion-exchange reaction time up to 3 h, more MoO3 molecules were in situ converted int BMO, resulting in more BMO nanosheets on the surface of MoO3 nanostructures (Figs. S2d and e). The TEM image in Fig. S2f shows that a small amount of void spaces exist in the center of MoO3 nanostructures. When the ion-exchange reaction time was further extended to 6 h, BMO nanosheets on the surface of MoO3 nanostructures became denser (Fig. S2g). Noted that hollow structures appeared in the interior of MoO3 nanostructures (Fig. S2h). The TEM image in Fig. S2i further proves this result. Based on these observations, the possible formation mechanism of hollow BMO nanostructures is proposed. As shown in Fig. S2j, MoO3 molecules on the surface of MoO3 nanostructures are gradually hydrated to generate H2MoO4 molecules during the hydrothermal treatment (eq. (2)), causing the surface of MoO3 nanostructures to be etched to form defect sites. These defects serve as active sites for subsequent nucleation and axial growth of BMO nanosheets by the ion-exchange reaction between H2MoO4 and Bi(NO3)3 (eqs. (3)–(5)). With increasing the reaction time, more and more MoO3 are in situ converted into BMO. At the same time, the interior of MoO3 nanostructures is gradually dissolved by the inside-out diffusion and subsequent ion-exchange reaction, resulting in the appearance of void spaces. Finally, MoO3 is completely transformed into BMO and the region of void spaces is also gradually be enlarged, forming the resulting hollow BMO nanostructures.

After doping N in BMO, the SEM images of the obtained hollow N-BMO nanostructures are shown in Fig. 2d, e. They still maintain hollow nanostructures. The TEM image in Fig. 2f further confirms this result. The thickness of N-BMO nanosheets ranges from 10 nm to 30 nm, as shown in Fig. 3 a. The high-resolution TEM image exhibits clear lattice fringes with d-spacing of 0.32 nm (Fig. 3b), which correspond to the (131) plane of orthorhombic BMO crystal (inset in Fig. 3b) [27].

Fig. S3 presents the TEM image of GQDs, which indicates that they are well dispersed and their sizes range from 1 nm to 5 nm. Fig. 2g-i shows the SEM images and TEM images of the N-BMO@GQD-2 obtained by decorating GQDs on the surface of hollow N-BMO nanostructures. Its morphology and microstructure are similar to those of hollow N-BMO nanostructures. The magnified TEM image confirms the presence of GQDs on the surface of N-BMO nanosheets (Fig. 3c), and the size of GQDs is 1–5 nm, as shown in Fig. 3d. It is noted that the surface decoration of conductive GQDs on N-BMO nanosheets has a capability of regulating the charge distribution at N-BMO/GQD interface, which will accelerate the interfacial reaction kinetics as well as maintain the structural integrity. Moreover, the high-resolution TEM image also shows lattice fringes with an interplanar distance of 0.32 nm, which are ascribed to the (131) plane of BMO (inset in Fig. 3d). The scanning transition electron microscopy (STEM) image and corresponding elemental mappings in Fig. 3e show the homogeneous distribution of N, Bi, Mo, O, and C elements in the N-BMO@GQD-2, further confirming the successful N doping in the BMO as well as the surface decoration of GQDs on the surface of hollow N-BMO nanostructures.

Fig. 4 a shows the XRD patterns of hollow BMO, N-BMO, and N-BMO@GQD-2. For hollow BMO nanostructures, its XRD pattern shows obvious diffraction peaks at 2θ = 28.3°, 32.5°, 46.7°, 55.3°, and 58.4°, respectively, all of which are indexed to the (131), (200), (202), (331), and (262) crystal planes of orthorhombic BMO (JCPDS No. 21–0102) [28,29]. After doping N in BMO, the XRD pattern of hollow N-BMO nanostructures exhibits similar diffraction peaks to hollow BMO nanostructures and no other diffraction peaks are detected, suggesting the absence of new phases during the N doping treatment. This result indicates that N atoms have been successfully incorporated into BMO crystal lattices. For the XRD pattern of N-BMO@GQD-2, in addition to diffraction peaks of BMO, no diffraction peaks of GQDs are detected, which probably originates from low diffraction intensity of GQDs [30].

Fig. 4b illustrates the N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms of hollow BMO, N-BMO, and N-BMO@GQD-2, all of which demonstrate type-IV curves with porous structures [31]. The specific surface area of N-BMO@GQD-2 was determined to be 91 m2 g−1 using the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller method. This value is higher than that of hollow BMO (42 m2 g−1) and N-BMO (53 m2 g−1). The increased surface area of N-BMO@GQD-2 is beneficial for the exposure of more electroactive sites to electrolyte, thereby enhancing charge storage capacity. Moreover, the TGA plots of hollow N-BMO and N-BMO@GQD nanocomposites are presented in Fig. S4. The GQD contents in nanocomposites are determined to be 7.9 %, 16.1 %, and 27.2 % for the N-BMO@GQD-1, N-BMO@GQD-2, and N-BMO@GQD-3, respectively.

XPS measurements were utilized to examine the elemental valence states and surface electronic structures of these three samples. Fig. 4c illustrates the full XPS survey spectra of samples, all of which illustrate the Bi 4f, Mo 3d, and O 1s signals. It is noted that the signal peaks of N 1s and Mo 3p have partial overlap within binding energies of 395 eV–401 eV [32]. However, the peak intensity of N 1s and Mo 3p in both N-BMO and N-BMO@GQD-2 is higher than that of N 1s in BMO, indicating that N atoms are successfully doped into BMO via the nitridation treatment. Moreover, the N-BMO@GQD-2 displays a stronger C 1s peak than both N-BMO and BMO, which is ascribed to the surface decoration of GQDs. In the high-resolution Bi 4f XPS spectra (Fig. 4d), the BMO shows two strong Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2 peaks at binding energies of 159.6 and 164.9 eV, respectively, suggesting the presence of Bi3+ in BMO [33]. In contrast to the Bi 4f peaks of BMO, a slightly negative shift of 0.2–0.4 eV is observed for Bi 4f peaks of both N-BMO and N-BMO@GQD-2. This may be due to the partial replacement of O atoms by N atoms, which has caused a change in the electronic band structure of Bi atoms, probably increasing their electrochemical activity. Similar result was also reported in N–MnO2-x branch arrays [34]. Moreover, the Bi 4f peaks of N-BMO@GQD-2 shift to a higher binding energy compared to those of N-BMO. This positive shift at binding energy should be attributed to the imbalanced charge distribution at the N-BMO/GQD interface, generating the electric interaction between N-BMO and GQDs [35]. This will be beneficial to push fast charge migration at the N-BMO/GQD interface and boost the affinity between N-BMO and GQDs, thus accelerating interfacial charge transfer and enhancing the structural stability of N-BMO@GQD-2. Fig. 4e shows the high-resolution Mo 3d XPS spectra, in which the BMO exhibits two Mo 3d5/2 and Mo 3d3/2 peaks at binding energies of 232.9 and 235.8 eV, respectively, suggesting the presence of Mo6+ [36,37]. However, both N-BMO and N-BMO@GQD-2 demonstrate two new peaks at binding energies of 231.8 and 235.2 eV, respectively, which are attributed to Mo5+ [36,37]. The presence of Mo5+ indicates the partial reduction of Mo6+ during the nitridation treatment. Similar result was also reported in N-doped NiMoO4 nanostructures [33]. Moreover, the binding energies of Mo 3d peaks of both N-BMO and N-BMO@GQD-2 show the same shift tendency as those of Bi 4f peaks of both N-BMO and N-BMO@GQD-2. Fig. 4f shows the O 1s spectra of three samples, in which they all exhibit three deconvoluted peaks at 530.1, 531.2, and 532.6 eV, respectively, corresponding to the M − O (Mo–O and Bi–O), oxygen vacancy (Ov), and adsorbed H–O–H molecules [38]. Fig. 4g illustrates the N 1s spectra of both N-BMO and N-BMO@GQD-2. The peak at 397.7 eV belongs to N–Mo/N–Bi bonds, while the other peak at 398.9 eV corresponds to the N–H species. The appearance of N–H species should originate from the incomplete reaction of NH3 during the nitridation treatment [39]. Fig. 4h displays the C 1s spectra of N-BMO@GQD-2, which can be deconvoluted into three peaks assigned to C–C (284.8 eV), C–O (286.7 eV), and -O-CO (289.1 eV), respectively [40,41]. This result verifies that the surface of N-BMO has been successfully decorated with GQDs.

The electrochemical performance of as-synthesized N-BMO@GQD nanocomposites as electrodes for SCs was evaluated employing the three-electrode configuration. Fig. 5 a illustrates CV curves of BMO, N-BMO, N-BMO@GQD-1, N-BMO@GQD-2, and N-BMO@GQD-3 electrodes at a scan rate of 10 mV s−1 in a potential window of −0.2 V–0.6 V. All these electrodes exhibit battery-type electrochemical behaviors with typical redox pairs, which arise from the reversible conversion between Bi (Ⅲ) and Bi metal [9,42]. From these CV curves, it is also observed that the N-BMO electrode demonstrates a larger CV integral area as well as higher peak currents than the BMO electrode, implying an enhanced charge storage capacity after the N doping. Furthermore, all the N-BMO@GQD-1, N-BMO@GQD-2, and N-BMO@GQD-3 electrodes illustrate larger CV integral areas compared to the N-BMO electrode, indicating that the surface decoration of GQDs on N-BMO nanostructures has a capability of further increasing charge storage capacities of N-BMO@GQD electrodes. It is noted that the N-BMO@GQD-2 electrode has the highest capacity because it shows the largest CV integral area. Accordingly, the amount of GQDs in N-BMO@GQD electrodes is a critical factor in determining the charge storage capacity of electrodes.

Fig. 5b illustrates a comparison of galvanostatic discharge curves from various electrodes operating at 1 A g−1. Evidently, the duration of discharge for N-BMO@GQD electrodes surpasses that of both N-BMO and BMO electrodes, meaning higher capacities. Furthermore, the N-BMO@GQD-2 electrode delivers the highest capacity as a result of its extended discharge duration. These findings agree well with the CV analysis.

Fig. 5c presents CV curves of N-BMO@GQD-2 electrode at various scan rates. Additionally, Fig. S5 provides CV curves of other electrodes. It is observed that the shape of all these curves is well maintained within the entire scan rates ranging form 5–50 mV s−1, revealing good reversibility of electrode reactions [43]. Charge storage process of electrodes is investigated by fitting the linear relationship between peak response (i
p ) and scan rate (ν) via the formula of i
p = a ν
b [3,44]. The b value is ascertained by analyzing the slope of plot from log i
p vs. log ν. Fig. 5d shows calculated b values of BMO (0.52), N-BMO (0.57), N-BMO@GQD-1 (0.60), N-BMO@GQD-2 (0.64), and N-BMO@GQD-3 (0.66) electrodes. All of these b values fall within 0.5–1.0, suggesting the co-existence of diffusion-controlled and surface capacitive processes in these electrodes. To quantify the specific contributions from diffusion-controlled and surface capacitive processes, the formula of i (V) = k
1
ν + k
2
ν 1/2 is adopted, in which k
1
ν and k
2
ν 1/2 correspond to surface capacitive and diffusion-controlled contributions, respectively [3,44]. Fig. 5e shows the surface capacitive contribution for N-BMO@GQD-2 electrode at 5 mV s−1, which accounts for 17 % of total charge storage (orange shaded area). At a scan rate of 50 mV s−1, the surface capacitive contribution exhibits a further increase, reaching 39 % (Fig. 5f). Moreover, the surface capacitive contributions of BMO, N-BMO, N-BMO@GQD-1, and N-BMO@GQD-3 electrodes are 18 %, 21 %, 28 %, and 44 %, respectively, at 50 mV s−1 (Fig. S6). By comparison, the surface capacitive contributions of N-BMO@GQD electrodes surpass those of both BMO and N-BMO electrodes at the same scan rates. This result indicates that the surface decoration of GQDs endows N-BMO@GQD electrodes with fast charge storage kinetics, thereby improving the rate performance.

The GCD curves of N-BMO@GQD-2 together with other electrodes at various current densities are illustrated in Fig. 6 a and Fig. S7. The GCD curves of each electrode display obvious charge/discharge plateaus, indicative of Faradaic redox behaviors of these electrodes. The capacity values of electrodes can be calculated from their GCD curves. As plotted in Fig. 6b, the specific capacity of N-BMO electrode is 366 C g−1 at 1 A g−1, surpassing that of BMO electrode (241 C g−1). Especially, the capacity values of N-BMO@GQD-1, N-BMO@GQD-2, and N-BMO@GQD-3 at 1 A g−1 are 446, 572, and 499 C g−1, respectively, surpassing specific capacities of both BMO and N-BMO electrodes. Even at 10 A g−1, the capacity values of N-BMO@GQD-1, N-BMO@GQD-2, and N-BMO@GQD-3 still retain 317, 435, and 369 C g−1, respectively, while exhibiting corresponding capacity retention of 71 %, 76 %, and 74 %. However, the BMO and N-BMO electrodes deliver lower specific capacities of 128 and 214 C g−1 with respective capacity retention of 53 % and 61 %. Notably, the electrochemical performance of N-BMO@GQD-2 electrode is competitive with that of previously reported Bi2MoO6-based electrodes in terms of specific capabilities (Table S1). These findings fully demonstrate the feasibility of boosting electrochemical performance of Bi2MoO6-based EMs by combining the N doping with surface decoration of GQDs.

The cyclic stability tests of the BMO, N-BMO, and N-BMO@GQD-2 electrodes were performed, as illustrated in Fig. 6c. After 10000 cycles at 5 A g−1, the N-BMO electrode retains 83 % of its initial capacity. By contrast, the BMO electrode only retains 72 % of its initial capacity. Compared to the N-BMO and BMO electrodes, the N-BMO@GQD-2 electrode achieves a higher 91 % capacity retention, and its morphology hardly changes after cycles (Fig. S8), demonstrating good long term cyclic stability.

EIS measurements were employed to evaluate charge storage kinetic behavior of different electrodes, as illustrated in Fig. 6d. The inset in Fig. 6d displays the equivalent circuit diagram that consists of the internal resistance (Rs), charge transfer resistance (Rct), constant phase element (CPE), and Warburg resistance (Zw) components. The detailed EIS data are presented in Table S2. It is evident that the N-BMO electrode exhibits lower Rs of 2.81 Ω and Rct of 1.67 Ω than the BMO electrode (Rs = 3.09 Ω and Rct = 2.01 Ω), indicating that the N doping in BMO enables enhanced electrical conductivity as well as promote fast charge transfer. Moreover, the N-BMO@GQD-1, N-BMO@GQD-2, and N-BMO@GQD-3 electrodes exhibit smaller Rs (2.37, 1.78, and 1.57 Ω, respectively) and Rct (1.23, 0.91, and 0.77 Ω, respectively) with respect to the N-BMO electrode. Therefore, the surface decoration of GQDs on the surface of N-BMO ensures higher electrical conductivity and charge transfer efficiency.

To further elucidate the positive effect of the N doping as well as surface decoration of GQDs on boosting charge storage performance of N-BMO@GQD, density functional theory (DFT) calculations were conducted. The optimized ball-and-stick models of the BMO, N-BMO, and N-BMO@GQD are presented in Fig. S9. First, the calculated electronic band structures of these three materials are shown in Fig. 7 a-c, in which the N-BMO exhibits a narrower band gap of 2.136 eV than that the pristine BMO (2.435 eV), confirming that the N doping in BMO increases the electrical conductivity. After decorating GQDs onto N-BMO, the obtained N-BMO@GQD shows a zero band gap with metallic nature, indicating that the introduction of GQDs further enhances the electrical conductivity. These results are also further proved by total and partial DOS analyses in Fig. 7d-f, which are consistent with the above EIS analysis. Fig. 7g shows a differential charge density at the N-BMO/GQD interface, revealing an obvious charge redistribution. A charge accumulation region is observed at GQD side, while a charge depletion region is located at N-BMO side. Therefore, a charge transfer occurs from N-BMO side to GQD side. Moreover, the planar-averaged electron density difference across the N-BMO/GQD interface again reveals the interfacial charge distribution and the presence of charge transferring from N-BMO side to GQD side. To shed light on electron dynamics at the N-BMO/GQD interface, Fig. 7g, h presents the calculated work functions (WF) of both N-BMO and GQDs. As can be seen, the WF of N-BMO and GQDs are 3.215 eV and 4.763 eV, respectively. Thus, when both N-BMO and GQDs contact each other and form a heterostructure, electrons will automatically migrate from N-BMO to GQD due to the difference in their WF until their respective Fermi levels reach equilibrium (Fig. 7j). Accordingly, a new charge distribution occurs at the interface between positively charged N-BMO and negatively charged GQDs. Simultaneously, a induced built-in electric field appears at the N-BMO/GQD interface, promoting interfacial electron transfer and enhancing electrode reaction kinetics. Additionally, the negatively charged GQDs are able to strongly attract K+ cations in KOH electrolyte, favoring more surface adsorption of K+. On the other hand, the N-BMO with a positive charge exhibits a pronounced electrostatic affinity towards OH− anions, resulting in fast diffusion and adequate utilization of OH−. These findings reveal that the combination of N-BMO and GQDs leads to a synergistic effect on charge storage, which ultimately improves the electrochemical performance of N-BMO@GQD electrodes.

Based on above mentioned results, it is believed that the N doping and surface decoration of GQDs together contribute to improving charge storage performance of N-BMO@GQD electrodes. On one hand, the N doping in BMO enhances the electrical conductivity of BMO by reducing its band gap, leading to fast charge transportation and high electrochemical activity. On the other hand, the surface decoration of GQDs with superior conductivity and high specific surface area further boosts electrical conductivity as well as provides more electrochemical sites. In particular, the charge redistribution occurs at the N-BMO/GQD interface due to their different WF, which is beneficial for more surface capacitive charge storage. Meanwhile, the generated built-in electric field facilitates fast interfacial charge transfer, accelerating electrochemical reaction kinetics. Therefore, decorating GQDs on the surface of N-BMO is beneficial for exerting the synergistic charge storage effect of these two active components and enhancing charge storage performance of N-BMO@GQD electrodes.

To estimate the real application of the obtained N-BMO@GQD electrode materials, an assembled two-electrode ASC device is schematically illustrated in Fig. 8 a, where the N-BMO@GQD-2 and N-CNTs are used as positive electrode and negative electrode, respectively, while a 2 M KOH solution is employed as electrolyte. Moreover, the CV and GCD curves of the N-CNT electrode are shown in Fig. S10. All these CV curves display a rectangular-like shape at 5–100 mV s−1 within −1.0-0 V (Fig. S10a), revealing capacitor-type charge storage behavior. Their GCD curves exhibit a highly symmetrical triangular-like shape at 1–10 A g−1 (Fig. S10b), indicating reversible charge storage feature. Fig. 8b shows the CV curves of N-BMO@GQD-2 and N-CNT electrodes working within −0.2–0.6 V and −1.0-0 V at 5 mV s−1. From these two CV curves, an operating voltage window of 0–1.6 V is feasible for the assembled N-BMO@GQD||N-CNTs device. A series of CV curves were measured in various voltage windows at 10 mV s−1 to further evaluate the optimal voltage window. As shown in Fig. 8c, the polarization phenomenon that is related to related to electrolysis of water appears in CV curves of this device when the operating voltage windows are expanded to 0–1.7 V and 0–1.8 V (indicated by red circles) [45]. In addition, the GCD curve of this device measured at 2 A g−1 becomes less symmetric if the voltage window exceeds 0–1.6 V (Fig. 8d). These results confirm that the working voltage of 1.6 V can be identified to assess charge storage capability of this assembled device. Fig. 8e shows CV curves of this device scanned at various rates within 0–1.6 V. A quasi-rectangular shape is observed within 0–0.8 V, while the redox pair appears within 0.8–1.6 V. This indicates that the device inherits advantages of Faradaic redox-type positive electrode and electric-double layer-based negative electrode [46]. Fig. 8f illustrates highly symmetrical GCD curves, indicating high Coulombic efficiency and good electrochemical reversibility for this device. The calculated specific capacities are 203, 197, 186, 170, and 157 C g−1 at 1–10 A g−1, respectively (Fig. 8g). The cyclic stability of this device is also evaluated. As shown in Fig. 8h the ASC device can still retain 93 % of its initial capacity as well as 94 % Coulombic efficiency after 10000 cycles at 5 A g−1.

The Ragone plots of the ASC device are shown in Fig. 9 a, alongside Ragone plots of previously documented BMO-based ASC devices. Our device deliver a favorable energy density of 45.2 Wh kg−1 at 801 W kg−1. It can even retains 34.8 Wh kg−1 at 7980 W kg−1. The energy densities achieved by our ASC device are competitive with those of previously documented two-electrode devices based on BMO, including BMO||BMO (45.6 Wh kg−1 at 989 W kg−1) [47], N-doped carbon dots (NCD)/BMO||NCD/BMO (44.2 Wh kg−1 at 720 W kg−1) [48], carbon sphere (CS)@BMO|CS@BMO (10.8 Wh kg−1 at 410 W kg−1) [9], BMO/polyaniline (PANI)||activated carbon (AC) (31.9 Wh kg−1 at 400 W kg−1) [49], and Co0.5Ni0.5MoO4@NiCo-LDH||BMO (41.5 Wh kg−1 at 369 W kg−1) [50]. Moreover, in order to meet the requirements for practical application, two such ASC devices are connected in series to successfully light up twenty one orange red light-emitting diodes for at least 6 min (Fig. 9b and c), suggesting a great promise for real-world applications in ESDs.

In this study, we have demonstrated a feasible strategy for constructing N-BMO@GQD nanocomposites by decorating GQDs on the surface of N-BMO hollow nanostructures. Experimental and DFT calculation results manifest that the N doping in BMO is beneficial to increasing the electrical conductivity of BMO by reducing its electronic energy band gap. Moreover, the surface decoration of GQDs on N-BMO further enhances the electrical conductivity while providing more electroactive sites. Especially, the Fermi-level difference between N-BMO and GQD induces the formation of electric field at the N-BMO/GQD interface, triggering fast ion/electron migration at the interface, speeding up electrode reaction kinetics, thereby enabling enhanced charge storage capability. As expected, the N-BMO@GQD electrodes demonstrate substantially enhanced specific capacities and cyclic stability in comparison with the BMO electrode. Moreover, the assembled ASC device with N-BMO@GQD positive electrode deliver favorable energy densities. This approach of creating N-BMO@GQD nanocomposites may be applied to develop other advanced transition-metal oxide-based electrode materials for further applications in SCs.
